about.heading: A Lifetime of Innovation in High-Voltage Cable Engineering
about.lead: Prof. Dr.-Ing. habil. Heinrich Brakelmann is one of the world's foremost authorities on high-voltage power cable systems — combining five decades of academic research with hands-on delivery of more than 110 major energy infrastructure projects.
about.bio1: He studied electrical engineering at RWTH Aachen (Diploma 1971) and completed his doctoral thesis on circuit breakers in 1973. He then joined Felten & Guilleaume Energietechnik AG in Cologne, leading an R&D unit focused on high-power cable technology — a role that grounded his theoretical expertise in large-scale industrial practice.
about.bio2: In 1977, he joined the University of Duisburg as chief engineer, earned his habilitation in 1985, was appointed apl. professor in 1992, and became full professor in 1994. At the Institute for Energy Transport and Storage (IW/ETS), he shaped the discipline over two decades. Now Professor emeritus, he continues to publish and consult at the frontier of the field.
about.bio3: He is author of approximately 260 technical publications and four books in the field of cable technology as well as high-voltage and high-current engineering. Through BCC Cable Consulting, he brings this unmatched combination of academic depth and project experience to clients worldwide — from offshore wind farm developers to national grid operators. He serves on the advisory board of the "Netzplattform" of the Federal Ministry for Economics and Energy (BMWi) for "Sustainable Grids".
about.award1Desc: For "Rating of Underground Power Cables with Boundary Temperature Restrictions" — IEEE Transactions on Power Delivery
about.award2Desc: VDE/ETG Award for outstanding contributions to power cable technology
about.award3Desc: Long-standing member of CIGRE WG 21-02 (current ratings), WG B1-05 (transient effects on long cables), WG B1.23 (EMF impact on cable ratings), WG B1.41 (long-term soil performance), SC 21 (cables), and German working group AK 411.1.1
about.affiliation: Professor emeritus, Institute for Energy Transport and Storage (IW/ETS), Faculty of Engineering Sciences, University of Duisburg-Essen
about.credentialDepartment: Institute for Energy Transport and Storage (IW/ETS)

projects.heading: 110+ Projects Delivered
projects.subheading: A selection of representative engagements across offshore and onshore cable systems.
projects.confidentialityNote: Project details available upon request — client confidentiality applies.

selfShieldingHV.title: Self-Shielding HV Cables — IES Cable Development
selfShieldingHV.description: Extreme magnetic field requirements near cables necessitated a new approach. Following a BCC proposal, cable manufacturer nkt cables (Cologne) developed the IES cable (Integrated Electromagnetic Shielding). The worldwide first 150-kV IES cable installation (3×500 mm² Cu, 200 MVA) was executed by TSO Liandon in Amsterdam. BCC conducted measurements and numerical validation — both matched closely, confirming the excellent shielding performance.

rinikenSwitzerland.title: Riniken, Switzerland — 380 kV Underground Cable Section
rinikenSwitzerland.description: A 1.5 km section of a 40 km 380-kV overhead line route had been blocked by court objections for 20 years. Initial TSO and manufacturer studies concluded cabling was unfeasible. Three BCC studies on thermal, mechanical, constructional, operational, and electromagnetic aspects — incorporating modern 380-kV cables and new installation techniques — provided the technical basis for the Swiss Federal Court ruling. BCC continues to support the TSO through planning and permitting.

hotSpotDistrictHeating.title: Hot-Spot in 380 kV Cable Trench with District Heating Pipes
hotSpotDistrictHeating.description: A 380-kV cable route required co-routing with 110-kV cables and two district heating pipes — a severe thermal challenge. BCC solved the problem through two measures: embedding the 380-kV cables in a specially developed high-conductivity concrete (initiated and supported by BCC with HeidelbergCement), and inserting a heat insulation plate between the cables and heating pipes. BCC executed time-dependent thermal analysis using a custom FEM tool adapted for nonlinear cable installations.

hvCables.title: High-Voltage Cable Systems
hvCables.description: Construction, optimization, and system integration of high-voltage cable systems. From design through commissioning, we deliver reliable solutions for onshore and submarine power transmission.

offshore.title: Offshore Wind Park Cabling
offshore.description: Inpark grid layout and connection to the transmission grid for offshore wind farms. Comprehensive cabling concepts that optimize cost, reliability, and power delivery.

thermal.title: Cable Analysis & Rating
thermal.description: Thermal and electrical analysis of cable and overhead line systems. Transient and stationary current ratings, switching surges, lightning impacts, and heating behavior — precise modeling for safe operation at maximum capacity.

emf.title: EMF & Magnetic Shielding
emf.description: Analysis of electric, magnetic, and thermal fields around cable systems. Expert assessment of EMF exposure and design of effective shielding measures.

layout.title: Wind Park Cable Layout Optimization
layout.description: Optimized cabling layout with respect to technology, economics, and reliability. Minimizing cable lengths and losses while maximizing energy yield.

monitoring.title: Monitoring & Forecasting Systems
monitoring.description: Intelligent monitoring with predictive forecasting for cable infrastructure. Real-time condition assessment and proactive maintenance planning for critical assets.

book.title: Erdkabel für den Netzausbau
book.subtitle: BoD-Verlag, 2019
book.description: By 2030, Germany has planned more than 9,000 km of high-voltage grid corridors – yet by early 2019, only around one eighth had been realised. Public opposition to overhead lines has led to significantly relaxed regulations for underground cabling. How can this grid expansion be achieved reliably, sustainably, and cost-effectively? Can underground cabling – full or partial – speed up approvals and reduce the planned investment costs of over €50 billion? This book presents the technical, economic, and ecological parameters of this societal challenge in accessible terms, and proposes innovative solutions with a focus on partial cabling in critical sections.
